key_insight: Graph Neural Differential Equations can be trained on small sparse random graphs and transferred zero-shot to larger graphs because their dynamics converge to well-posed graphon-limit systems with provable rates.
engineering_relevance: This gives a theoretical foundation for scaling continuous-time graph models across problem sizes without retraining, which is useful for large-network simulation, control, and scientific machine learning.
method: Define Graphon Neural Differential Equations and adjoint Graphon-NDEs as infinite-node limits of forward and backward GNDE dynamics.
Prove trajectory-wise convergence of GNDEs to graphon limits at rate O((α_n n)^-1/2) up to logarithmic factors.
Analyze DTO vs OTD under explicit Euler and show asymptotic consistency with hidden-state and gradient discrepancies of O(1/M) and O(1/M^2).
evidence_signal: Experiments on HSBM and tent graphons, plus zero-shot transfer across four graphon classes, support the predicted convergence and deployment behavior.
watch_item: The guarantees depend on sparse random graph assumptions, graphon sampling structure, and explicit Euler discretization, so real-world graphs may need careful validation before zero-shot transfer.
paper_title: Zero-Shot Size Transfer for Neural ODEs on Sparse Random Graphs: Graphon Limits and Adjoint Convergence
